coredata.dc:title: Pectin-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitors for N80 steel in 1 M sulfuric acid solution

coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using pectin polysaccharide as the carbon source, with 1-hexylamine, 1,2-diaminocyclohexane, and diethylenetriamine as individual nitrogen sources. The chemical composition, fluorescence properties, and morphology of the synthesized N-CDs were characterized. Their corrosion inhibition performance on N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4 was evaluated using electrochemical analyses and weight loss experiments. Tafel and EIS analyses demonstrated that at a concentration of 200 mg/L, the maximum inhibition efficiencies of PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs, synthesized using monoamine, diamine, and triamine as nitrogen sources, were 75.3 %, 90.1 %, and 95.4 %, respectively. The inhibition efficiency increased with the total nitrogen and pyrrolic N content in the N-CDs, corresponding to the choice of nitrogen source. Weight loss experiments further confirmed these trends. Additionally, the N-CDs were found to act as mixed-type inhibitors with a predominant anodic inhibition effect and are classified as predominantly chemisorptive. The adsorption behavior adhered to the Langmuir adsorption model, where coordination complexes formed between pyrrolic N and metal Fe increase the surface coverage of the adsorption film, thereby enhancing corrosion resistance.

head.ce:title: Pectin-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitors for N80 steel in 1 M sulfuric acid solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using pectin polysaccharide as the carbon source, with 1-hexylamine, 1,2-diaminocyclohexane, and diethylenetriamine as individual nitrogen sources. The chemical composition, fluorescence properties, and morphology of the synthesized N-CDs were characterized. Their corrosion inhibition performance on N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4 was evaluated using electrochemical analyses and weight loss experiments. Tafel and EIS analyses demonstrated that at a concentration of 200 mg/L, the maximum inhibition efficiencies of PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs, synthesized using monoamine, diamine, and triamine as nitrogen sources, were 75.3 %, 90.1 %, and 95.4 %, respectively. The inhibition efficiency increased with the total nitrogen and pyrrolic N content in the N-CDs, corresponding to the choice of nitrogen source. Weight loss experiments further confirmed these trends. Additionally, the N-CDs were found to act as mixed-type inhibitors with a predominant anodic inhibition effect and are classified as predominantly chemisorptive. The adsorption behavior adhered to the Langmuir adsorption model, where coordination complexes formed between pyrrolic N and metal Fe increase the surface coverage of the adsorption film, thereby enhancing corrosion resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal materials play a vital role in modern industrial production. Among them, carbon steel is widely recognized for its low cost, excellent mechanical properties, and superior thermal and electrical conductivity, making it a preferred material for engineers in industries such as shipbuilding, chemical engineering, construction, oil and gas pipelines, and drilling casings [1,2]. However, prolonged use of carbon steel often leads to surface corrosion and scaling, which can significantly degrade its mechanical strength and shorten the operational lifespan of pipes and equipment [3]. To address this issue, high-concentration acid pickling agents, such as sulfuric and hydrochloric acids, are commonly employed to remove scale and rust from carbon steel [4,5]. Unfortunately, carbon steel is highly susceptible to chemical and electrochemical corrosion in such acidic environments, resulting in structural damage that may lead to equipment failure, reduced production efficiency, and even serious safety risks [6]. Corrosion is a widespread and irreversible problem, with global economic losses exceeding 4 trillion USD annually [7]. In industrialized nations, such losses are estimated to represent 2 %-4 % of GDP [8–10]. Therefore, the development of effective corrosion protection technologies for metals is of paramount importance.

Corrosion prevention strategies are well-established and include the use of corrosion-resistant alloy steels, cathodic and anodic protection, protective coatings, and corrosion inhibitors [11]. Among these, corrosion inhibitors have gained widespread attention due to their cost-effectiveness, versatility, ease of application, and proven efficiency [12,13]. Corrosion inhibitors function primarily by forming a protective layer on metal surfaces, thereby minimizing direct exposure to corrosive media and effectively reducing the rate of metal corrosion [14,15]. These inhibitors can be broadly categorized as inorganic or organic. While inorganic inhibitors such as chromates, molybdates, phosphates, and nitrites are cost-effective, their high toxicity poses significant environmental hazards [16]. Traditional organic inhibitors, despite being highly effective, are often limited by poor water solubility, complex synthesis processes, and high toxicity, making them environmentally problematic as well [17]. Green organic inhibitors, such as plant extracts [15,18] and amino acids, contain no toxic or hazardous groups, but their complex preparation methods and higher costs restrict their broader adoption [7]. With growing environmental awareness and increasing demand for sustainable solutions, the development of eco-friendly, water-soluble, cost-effective, and highly efficient corrosion inhibitors has become a key focus in corrosion protection research.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), a class of zero-dimensional materials with sizes smaller than 10 nm, have garnered significant attention in various cutting-edge fields. This interest stems from their unique properties, including strong fluorescence emission, abundant raw material sources, excellent water solubility, quantum size effects, a high density of surface functional groups, low biological toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [19–22]. These attributes have made CDs highly attractive for applications in bioimaging [23], energy storage [24], medical diagnostics [25], ion detection [26], and photocatalysis [27]. Moreover, CDs have demonstrated great potential in green corrosion protection [28], with their use in metal corrosion inhibition first reported in 2017 [29]. Since then, various strategies have been developed to enhance the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs, including doping with elements such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorus (P), and copper (Cu), as well as combining different dopants and molecular modifications [30–32]. Due to the similarity in atomic size between nitrogen and carbon, N-doping does not significantly alter the electronic structure or atomic environment of CDs, while simultaneously increasing active sites and enhancing fluorescence properties [33], making it a focal point of current research. For example, N-CDs synthesized via hydrothermal methods using citric acid as the carbon source and nitrogen sources such as ethylenediamine [31,34], aminoguanidine [35], urea [36], ammonia solution [37], thiourea [3], or L-histidine [38] have demonstrated corrosion inhibition efficiencies exceeding 90 % in acidic media, as summarized in Table S1. Further studies revealed that nitrogen is typically incorporated into N-CDs as pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N [39], where lone pair electrons facilitate the formation of coordination bonds with metal atoms, significantly enhancing adsorption capacity and surface coverage to effectively block corrosive media from attacking the metal surface [40]. These findings highlight the diversity of nitrogen sources available for N-CD synthesis. However, most studies focus on single nitrogen sources and primarily investigate the effects of synthesis conditions (e.g., temperature [41] and time [42]) on nitrogen doping and corrosion inhibition performance, leaving the systematic relationship between nitrogen source diversity and N-CD corrosion inhibition properties insufficiently explored. Additionally, variations in synthesis conditions and testing methods among different studies further complicate the comprehensive comparison and identification of universal trends. In this context, the present study systematically investigates how nitrogen source variation influences nitrogen doping type and ratio in N-CDs under fixed carbon source and synthesis conditions, aiming to elucidate their relationship with corrosion inhibition efficiency. By clarifying these trends, this study seeks to provide scientific guidance for selecting nitrogen sources to develop green and efficient N-CD-based corrosion inhibitors, thereby minimizing resource waste while enhancing the precision of material design.

Herein, pectin polysaccharides, rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, were selected as the carbon source, and three nitrogen sources, namely 1-hexylamine (monoamine), 1,2-diaminocyclohexane (diamine), or diethylenetriamine (triamine), were individually employed to synthesize novel functionalized N-CDs via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Pectin, as a renewable and environmentally friendly carbon source, aligns with the growing demand for sustainable materials in corrosion protection. Organic amines were chosen as nitrogen sources due to their high reactivity, and the incremental increase in the number of amino groups allows fine-tuning of the reaction activity, potentially influencing the total nitrogen content, nitrogen doping type distribution, and the proportions of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N in the N-CDs, thereby enhancing their corrosion inhibition performance on N80 steel. The corrosion inhibition performance of the N-CDs on N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4 at 60°C was evaluated, and the potential mechanisms of corrosion inhibition were explored. The synthesized N-CDs were comprehensively characterized using Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Their corrosion protection performance was further evaluated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Tafel polarization curves, weight loss measurements, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Langmuir adsorption isotherms, providing robust data to support the proposed inhibition mechanisms. By correlating nitrogen source diversity with the functional properties of the resulting N-CDs, this work offers novel insights into the rational design of green and high-performance corrosion inhibitors.

Pectin (galacturonic acid content ≥ 74.0 %, dry basis), 1-hexylamine (AR, 99 %), 1,2-diaminocyclohexane (AR, 97 %), and diethylenetriamine (AR, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, AR, 98 %) was obtained from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. N80 steel samples for weight loss measurements were cut to dimensions of 4.0 cm × 1.3 cm × 0.2 cm, and their chemical composition is as follows: 0.39 % C, 0.31 % Si, 1.59 % Mn, 0.019 % S, 0.014 % P, 0.037 % Cr, 0.015 % Ni, 0.007 % Cu, 0.020 % Mo, with Fe as the balance. For electrochemical tests, a cylindrical working electrode with an exposed surface area of 1 cm2 was used, while the remaining surfaces were sealed with epoxy resin. Deionized water (18.21 MΩ) was prepared using an ultrapure water system in the laboratory.

N-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method, involving the following key steps. First, 0.8 g of pectin was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, and 1.6 g of organic amine was added dropwise with stirring. The resulting mixture was then subjected to ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the resulting solution was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, with the water replaced every 4 h. Finally, the target product was obtained through vacuum drying. The organic amines used in this process were 1-hexylamine, 1,2-diaminocyclohexane, and diethylenetriamine, corresponding to the products PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs, respectively. The synthesis process of N-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

To evaluate the corrosion inhibition performance of the synthesized N-CDs including PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs on N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4, solutions with concentrations of 0, 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg/L were prepared. The 1 M H2SO4 solution was prepared by diluting concentrated sulfuric acid (98 %) with deionized water.

The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption properties of the N-CDs were measured using a TU-1900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi General Instrument, China) across the wavelength range of 200–800 nm, with a sampling interval of 1 nm and a fixed slit width of 5 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) characteristics were analyzed using an F-4700 fluorescence spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan). The elemental composition and chemical bonding states of the N-CDs were determined via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250, USA). Functional groups were identified using a NICOLET 6700 Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The morphology of the N-CDs was observed with a JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), and particle size distribution was analyzed using Nano Measure software.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using a CHI-660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China) to evaluate the corrosion behavior of N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4, both with and without N-CD inhibitors. A conventional three-electrode system was employed, consisting of an N80 steel working electrode (WE), a platinum plate counter electrode (CE), and a saturated calomel reference electrode (RE). Before testing, the N80 steel surfaces were polished sequentially with 400, 800, 1200, 2000, and 4000 grit sandpaper, rinsed with deionized water, air-dried, and used immediately. The open-circuit potential (OCP) was recorded for 60 min to ensure system stabilization, followed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements. EIS measurements were conducted across a frequency range of 105 Hz to 10-2 Hz, using a sinusoidal perturbation of 5 mV. The resulting data were analyzed with ZSimpWin software. PDP scans were conducted at a scan rate of 1 mV/s over a potential range of −300 mV to + 300 mV to obtain Tafel plots. All experiments were performed in triplicate under identical conditions, and the average results were reported.

ce:section.ce:label: 2.6 wt
ce:section.ce:para:
The corrosion inhibition efficiency of N-CDs on N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4 was evaluated using weight loss experiments. Initially, the N80 steel samples were degreased with acetone-soaked cotton, followed by cleaning with ethanol-soaked cotton. The samples were polished sequentially using 400, 800, 1200, 2000, and 4000 grit sandpaper to achieve a smooth surface. After polishing, the samples were immersed in anhydrous ethanol, air-dried with a cold air blower, and weighed three times on an analytical balance to calculate the average weight. The length, width, height, and diameter of the circular hole were measured with a vernier caliper, and the samples were stored in a desiccator until further use. The steel samples were immersed in 1 M H2SO4 solutions, with and without the three N-CD inhibitors, and maintained in a water bath at 60 °C for 24 h. After immersion, the samples were rinsed with deionized water, ultrasonically cleaned in anhydrous ethanol, dried with a cold air blower, and reweighed. Each test was performed in triplicate, and the average results were reported. The corrosion rate (C R) (g·m2·h−1) and inhibition efficiency (η 0) were determined using the following equations [43]:

where M 0 and M are the mass of the steel sample before and after immersion in the H2SO4 solution, g; S is the total exposed surface area, m2; t is the immersion time, h; C R0 and C Ri represent the corrosion rates without and with the inhibitor, respectively.

The surface corrosion morphology of N80 steel samples, immersed for 24 h in 1 M H2SO4 with and without 200 mg/L N-CD inhibitors, was observed using a MIRA3 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, TESCAN, Czech Republic). XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the elemental distribution on the corroded steel surface. Throughout the immersion period, the solution temperature was consistently maintained at 60 °C.

Fig. 2 illustrates the UV–Vis and PL spectra of three N-CDs synthesized from pectin polysaccharides and different nitrogen sources. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2a-c) display three distinct absorption bands. The shoulder peaks at 277–284 nm and 340–351 nm correspond to the n-π* transitions of CN and CO bonds, respectively, while the sharp peaks at 209 nm are attributed to the π-π* transitions of sp2 domains in CC bonds [2,44].

At selected excitation wavelengths, all synthesized N-CDs exhibit strong emission peaks. As the excitation wavelength increases from 340 nm to 420 nm, the emission peaks shift from 420 nm to 490 nm, showcasing the typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emission behavior of N-CDs [45]. The maximum excitation wavelengths for PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs are 400 nm, 360 nm, and 360 nm, respectively, corresponding to their strongest fluorescence intensities. Their corresponding emission wavelengths are 467 nm, 430 nm, and 440 nm, respectively. The variations in excitation and emission wavelengths among the N-CDs are attributed to differences in particle size, fluorescence properties, and the distribution of surface emissive sites [46]. Given the common application of 365 nm UV light for in situ fluorescence observations, the fluorescence of N-CDs was also recorded at this excitation wavelength [47]. As shown in the insets of Fig. 2a–c, N-CD solutions appear light yellow or colorless under daylight but emit blue fluorescence under UV light. This blue emission likely originates from CO functional groups on the N-CD surfaces and CN/CN bonds within their cores. Differences in fluorescence intensity may be attributed to varying concentrations of nitrogen-containing and carbonyl groups, where higher concentrations typically result in stronger fluorescence [44,48]. UV absorption may arise from energy stored in defect sites on the passivated surfaces of the CDs, which is released upon UV excitation and contributes to the fluorescence effect. Nitrogen doping enhances surface passivation, further increasing fluorescence efficiency [49,50]. The fluorescence properties of CDs are a key feature, indicating successful synthesis.

The elemental composition of the synthesized N-CDs was determined using XPS, with the results presented in Fig. 3 a–c and Table S2. By observation, the N-CDs primarily consist of C, N, and O, confirming successful nitrogen doping. The nitrogen content in PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs, synthesized from the same carbon source but different nitrogen sources, was 6.66 %, 10.00 %, and 17.29 %, respectively. PDA-CDs exhibited the highest nitrogen content, which is likely attributed to the greater number of amine groups in its nitrogen source. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s were analyzed to investigate the bonding configurations of C, N, and O within the N-CDs. The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 3d–l) reveal three distinct peaks at 284.8, 286.0, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO/CN, and NCO bonds, respectively [51–53]. The presence of amide bonds suggests that a condensation reaction occurred between the amino groups of organic amines and the carboxyl groups in the CDs [54]. The N1s spectra exhibit three peaks at 398.8, 399.8, and 401.4 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [55,56]. Pyrrolic N enhances the adsorption capacity and corrosion inhibition efficiency of N-CDs [49]. The O1s spectra display two peaks at 531.21 and 532.47 eV, corresponding to CO and COH/COC bonds [6,52]. These results indicate that the three N-CDs share similar elemental compositions and bonding configurations, with differences in the relative proportions of each bonding structure. Detailed data are provided in Tables S3 and S4. Notably, PDA-CDs exhibit the highest relative contents of NCO, pyrrolic N, and CO bonds, measuring 16.63 %, 77.68 %, and 62.69 %, respectively, which are significantly higher than those in PCA-CDs (2.66 %, 53.89 %, and 31.21 %) and PHA-CDs (4.91 %, 68.14 %, and 23.15 %). The absolute pyrrolic N content is highest in PDA-CDs at 13.43 %, compared to 5.39 % in PCA-CDs and 4.53 % in PHA-CDs. These differences in bonding structures likely contribute to the variations in fluorescence properties among the N-CDs [57], as well as their differing corrosion inhibition efficiencies.

Fig. S1 presents the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs, which were analyzed to characterize their surface functional groups and structural properties. A broad absorption band at 3394–3272 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH groups and NH vibrations from amines or amides [53]. The absorption peaks at 2971–2856 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH bonds [58]. Strong absorption peaks at 1656–1629 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO bonds in amide or carboxyl groups [59]. Peaks representing CN/CC and CN bonds appear at approximately 1585–1578 cm−1 [18] and 1454–1448 cm−1 [51], respectively, while the absorption peak for COO– is observed near 1383 cm−1 [52]. Additionally, absorption peaks in the ranges of 1346–1336 cm−1 and 1146–1049 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibrations of CO bonds [58]. These results align with the XPS analysis, further confirming the successful doping of nitrogen atoms into the carbon dot structure.

The morphology and lattice structures of the three N-CDs were characterized via TEM, with the sizes of 100 particles randomly measured to determine the average particle size. As shown in Fig. 4 , PHA-CDs exhibit an irregular spherical shape with slight aggregation and a broad size distribution (9.2–34.3 nm) and an average size of 18.8 nm. In contrast, PCA-CDs and PDA-CDs display quasi-spherical shapes, high dispersity, and no aggregation, with size distributions following a Gaussian pattern. Their sizes range from 1.6–4.3 nm and 0.9–3.1 nm, with average sizes of 3.1 nm and 1.9 nm, respectively. The narrower size distributions of PCA-CDs and PDA-CDs indicate higher surface energy, which enhances their adsorption onto carbon steel surfaces and promotes the formation of dense protective layers that inhibit contact between the steel and the corrosive medium [1]. The insets in Fig. 4a–c (HRTEM images) reveal that PHA-CDs lack distinct lattice fringes, indicative of an amorphous carbon structure. On the other hand, PCA-CDs and PDA-CDs show well-defined crystalline structures with lattice spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.20 nm, respectively, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100 facet) [51,60,61]. The slight difference in lattice spacing between the two types of N-CDs likely stems from variations in the molecular structures of their nitrogen sources, which affect the compactness of microcrystalline carbon stacking [62]. Additionally, based on the XPS analysis results, PDA-CDs, compared to PCA-CDs, contain a higher proportion of electronegative atoms (e.g., N and O). This increased electronegativity may reduce the energy required to form hydrogen bonds at the boundaries of the microcrystalline structures, thereby promoting a denser arrangement and resulting in a smaller interlayer spacing [63].

Fig. 5 shows the Tafel curves of N80 steel after immersion in 1 M H2SO4 solutions with varying concentrations of N-CDs at 60 °C for 24 h. Table 1 summarizes the parameters derived from the Tafel curves, including corrosion current density (I corr), corrosion potential (E corr), and the anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (β a and β c). The results clearly demonstrate that the addition of N-CDs significantly reduces the I corr of N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4. With increasing inhibitor concentration, the Tafel curves shift toward more negative potentials, and I corr progressively decreases, indicating that these inhibitors effectively mitigate the corrosion of N80 steel in this acidic medium. Moreover, the inhibition efficiency increases with higher N-CD concentrations. Compared to the blank solution, both β a and −β c values for N80 steel in the inhibited solutions are generally elevated, with a more pronounced increase in β a than in −β c. This suggests that the inhibitors suppress both anodic Fe dissolution and cathodic hydrogen evolution, classifying them as mixed-type inhibitors with a more pronounced effect on anodic metal dissolution [64]. Additionally, the similar shapes of the Tafel curves across all groups, including the blank control, indicate that the presence of N-CDs does not modify the corrosion mechanism of N80 steel in sulfuric acid. The I corr values were subsequently used in equations (3) to calculate the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η 1) [65], with the results presented in Table 1.

where I corr0 and I corr represent the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of N-CDs, respectively. The data indicate that PDA-CDs achieve the highest corrosion inhibition efficiency, exceeding 90 % at 100 mg/L and further increasing to 95.4 % at 200 mg/L. The inhibition performance of the three N-CDs follow the order: PDA-CDs > PCA-CDs > PHA-CDs. This trend correlates with the XPS analysis, which reveals differences in nitrogen content and bonding configurations, as well as the TEM analysis, which highlights variations in particle size and distribution. Specifically, N-CDs with higher pyrrolic nitrogen content, smaller particle sizes, and size distributions closer to a Gaussian pattern demonstrate superior corrosion inhibition efficiency.

Fig. 6 a–c and d–f show the Nyquist and Bode plots, respectively, of N80 carbon steel after immersion in 1 M H2SO4 solutions containing different concentrations of N-CDs at 60 °C for 24 h. The Nyquist plots exhibit typical capacitive semicircles, with their depressed shape attributed to the “dispersion effect” caused by the surface roughness and heterogeneity of the carbon steel [66–68]. The observation of similar semicircles in both the blank sample and samples with varying concentrations of the inhibitor suggests that the corrosion mechanism remains consistent, regardless of whether the inhibitor is present or absent [69]. Compared to the blank control, the capacitive arcs of the N-CD-treated samples exhibit significantly larger diameters, indicating that these inhibitors effectively impede the charge transfer process, thereby enhancing corrosion resistance [28]. Furthermore, as the N-CD concentration increases, the capacitive arc diameters expand significantly, likely due to the adsorption of more inhibitor molecules onto the carbon steel surface, forming a dense film that blocks direct contact between the metal and the corrosive medium [28,31]. Correspondingly, the low-frequency impedance modulus and the maximum phase angle in the Bode plots also increase with higher N-CD concentrations, further corroborating that the corrosion inhibition efficiency improves as the inhibitor concentration rises [70,71].

To further analyze the EIS results, the data for the samples without and with N-CDs were fitted using the equivalent circuit models shown in Fig. 6g and h [37]. In these models, L, R s, R f, and R ct represent inductance, solution resistance, N-CDs film resistance, and charge transfer resistance, respectively, while Q f and Q dl represent the N-CDs film capacitance and double-layer capacitance. The fitted parameters, along with the goodness of fit (χ2), polarization resistance (R p), and corrosion inhibition efficiency (η 2), are presented in Table 2 . The values of R p, R p0, and η 2 were calculated using Eqs. (4)–(6) [37].

R
p 0
/
R p
× 100 %
where R p0 and R p represent the polarization resistances in the absence and presence of N-CDs, respectively. The fitted curves closely match the experimental data, with χ2 values below 5 × 10-4, indicating that the chosen equivalent circuit models are valid. As seen in Table 2, the R ct values increase with inhibitor concentration, indicating successful adsorption of N-CDs on the carbon steel surface, which effectively inhibits the charge transfer process of anodic Fe dissolution [43]. Meanwhile, the decreasing Q dl values indicate that the inhibitors displace pre-adsorbed water molecules on the carbon steel surface, expanding the adsorbed film coverage [59]. This expansion reduces the active corrosion area and enhances the inhibition effect. Additionally, at the same concentration, PDA-CDs show significantly higher R ct values than PCA-CDs and PHA-CDs, with similar trends observed for R p. Conversely, the Q dl values follow the order PDA-CDs < PCA-CDs < PHA-CDs, further indicating that PDA-CDs provide the best corrosion inhibition among the three N-CDs. This conclusion is supported by the inhibition efficiency data, which aligns with the Tafel analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: 3.4 wt
ce:section.ce:para: The weight loss method provides a simple yet reliable means to evaluate corrosion rates and inhibition efficiencies. The corrosion behavior of N80 steel at 60 °C in 1 M H2SO4, without and with 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg/L of PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, or PDA-CDs, was evaluated using the weight loss method, and the corresponding corrosion rates and inhibition efficiencies were determined. The results are presented in Fig. 7 and Table S5. It is evident that all three N-CDs significantly reduce the corrosion rate of carbon steel, with inhibition improving as concentration increases, although the rate of improvement tapers off at higher concentrations. At 200 mg/L, the corrosion rates of carbon steel in 1 M H2SO4 containing PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs are 15.23 g·m−2·h−1, 6.34 g·m−2·h−1, and 3.65 g·m−2·h−1, respectively, representing reductions of 74.0 %, 89.2 %, and 93.8 % relative to the blank solution. These results indicate that PDA-CDs provide the highest corrosion inhibition, followed by PCA-CDs and PHA-CDs.

Fig. 8 presents SEM images of the N80 steel surface after 24 h immersion in 1 M H2SO4 at 60 °C, without and with 200 mg/L of PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs, respectively. N80 steel immersed in uninhibited 1 M H2SO4 exhibits severe corrosion, marked by distinct corrosion grooves and abundant surface corrosion products. The addition of N-CDs significantly reduces corrosion grooves and noticeably enhances surface smoothness. This improvement can be attributed to the formation of a protective adsorption film that minimizes direct contact between the corrosive medium and the metal surface. The protective performance varies among the N-CD types: PHA-CDs reduce surface corrosion but cause pitting to coalesce into larger areas, especially along polishing lines. By comparison, PCA-CDs produce more scattered pitting corrosion without significant coalescence into larger areas. PDA-CDs show the most substantial improvement, with only a few isolated corrosion spots visible, likely due to the formation of a more robust and extensive adsorption film. Among the three N-CD inhibitors, PDA-CDs provide the most effective protection, followed by PCA-CDs, with PHA-CDs showing the least effectiveness. These findings align with the electrochemical and weight loss test results.

To determine the adsorption type of N-CDs on the surface of N80 steel, XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the elemental composition and bonding states of the steel surface after corrosion tests, as illustrated in Fig. 9 and Table S6. The steel surface immersed in sulfuric acid without N-CDs displayed noticeable traces of C, O, and Fe. In contrast, the steel surface treated with N-CDs exhibited additional distinct N peaks, confirming the successful adsorption of N-CDs onto the N80 steel surface [72]. Furthermore, compared to the blank control group (47.63 % O and 15.17 % Fe), the addition of N-CDs significantly reduced the oxygen and iron content on the steel surface. Specifically, the O and Fe contents decreased to 39.50 % and 8.91 % for PHA-CDs, 36.96 % and 8.00 % for PCA-CDs, and 33.76 % and 7.20 % for PDA-CDs, respectively. These findings indicate a decrease in corrosion products and effective inhibition of the corrosion process [16]. High-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 9b–d and Fig. S2) revealed three distinct peaks in the C1s spectrum at 284.80, 285.89, and 288.16 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, and CO bonds, respectively. For the N1s spectrum, peaks at 398.81, 399.61, and 400.57 eV were assigned to unprotonated nitrogen atoms (CN) in N-CDs, N–Fe coordination bonds formed between nitrogen and the steel surface, and protonated nitrogen atoms (N+H), respectively [12,55]. These results suggest that the adsorption mechanism of N-CDs on the steel surface involves a combination of physical and chemical adsorption [1,31,59]. Similarly, the O1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 529.26, 530.74, and 532.25 eV, corresponding to O2– (from FeO/Fe2O3), CO, and CO bonds, respectively [12,30,73]. In the Fe2p high-resolution spectrum, peaks at 707.23 and 721.03 eV were attributed to metallic Fe, while peaks at 709.96 and 723.39 eV corresponded to FeO, 711.48 and 725.04 eV to Fe2O3, and 713.52 and 726.94 eV to FeOOH/N–Fe, with additional satellite peaks characteristic of Fe2p [74]. The presence of N–Fe coordination bonds and metallic Fe further confirms the effective protection of the steel surface [66].

To gain a deeper understanding of the adsorption mechanism of N-CDs on carbon steel, several adsorption isotherm models, including Langmuir, Temkin, El-Awady, Flory-Huggins, and Freundlich, were applied to fit the results obtained from weight loss, Tafel, and EIS measurements [6,66]. The linear regression coefficients (R2) for each model are presented in Table S7 and Fig. 10 . Based on results from all three corrosion performance tests, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model yields R2 values closest to 1, indicating the most accurate description of the adsorption mechanism on the carbon steel surface. The model is described by the following equation [51]:
(7)
C / θ = 1 /
K
ads
+ C
where C represents the concentration of the N-CDs inhibitor, θ denotes the surface coverage, and K ads is the adsorption equilibrium constant. Generally, a larger K ads value indicates stronger adsorption performance [56,75]. As shown in Fig. 10, the average K ads values for PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs are 56.12 L/g, 89.44 L/g, and 148.76 L/g, respectively, suggesting that PDA-CDs exhibit the strongest adsorption capability. Furthermore, the standard Gibbs free energy of adsorption (ΔG 0 ads) can be used to determine the specific type of adsorption. ΔG 0 ads values for physisorption, chemisorption, and mixed adsorption typically fall within ranges > -20 kJ/mol, < −40 kJ/mol, and −20 kJ/mol to −40 kJ/mol, respectively. The calculation formula is as follows [71]:
(8)
Δ
G
ads
0
= - R T I n
1000
K
ads
where R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1K−1) and T is the absolute temperature (333 K in this case). The average ΔG 0 ads values for PHA-CDs, PCA-CDs, and PDA-CDs are −30.07 kJ/mol, −31.43 kJ/mol, and –32.97 kJ/mol, respectively. These values indicate that all three N-CDs interact with the metal surface through mixed adsorption, consistent with literature data summarized in Table S1. Among the three N-CDs, PDA-CDs exhibit relatively stronger chemisorption.

The corrosion mechanism in sulfuric acid solution and the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs are depicted in Fig. 11 , based on the above discussion. Fig. 11a illustrates the electrochemical corrosion process of carbon steel in sulfuric acid solution without N-CDs. This process involves anodic and cathodic reactions, specifically Fe → Fe2+ + 2e- and 2H+ + 2e- → H2↑, which accelerate the dissolution of carbon steel in acidic media, leading to severe surface corrosion [16,76].

According to the adsorption mechanism analysis, the adsorption of N-CDs on the carbon steel surface involves mixed adsorption, combining physical and chemical adsorption. Physical adsorption arises from the abundant functional groups on the N-CDs surface (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and amide groups) and nanoscale effects. These factors enable N-CDs to aggregate and spread over the metal surface through electrostatic and van der Waals forces, forming a protective layer that reduces the exposed area of carbon steel in the acidic medium [40,77]. Chemical adsorption, on the other hand, is attributed to the formation of coordination bonds between heteroatoms in N-CDs and the carbon steel surface. The lone pairs on the pyridine N atoms in N-CDs can readily fill the unoccupied 3d orbitals of Fe atoms, forming coordination bonds between N atoms and Fe [55,59,78]. Additionally, pyrrole N atoms in N-CDs, due to their “excess π-electrons”, are susceptible to electrophilic attack, leading to the formation of π-coordination complexes with Fe. This promotes the parallel adsorption of pyrrole rings onto the carbon steel surface, enhancing the surface coverage of N-CDs, which is crucial for improving corrosion inhibition performance [37,79]. As shown in Fig. 11b, this mixed adsorption enables N-CDs to form an adsorption film on the carbon steel surface, preventing direct contact between the corrosive medium and the metal. Consequently, the dissolution of Fe atoms at the anode is suppressed, electron transfer is significantly reduced, and the cathodic reduction of hydrogen is slowed, effectively inhibiting electrochemical corrosion.

Based on TEM, XPS, and Langmuir adsorption analyses, PDA-CDs exhibit the smallest particle size (with a nearly Gaussian distribution), the highest total nitrogen and pyrrole N content, and the largest K ads and −ΔG 0 ads values among the three N-CDs. This suggests that PDA-CDs form a denser and stronger protective film, resulting in superior corrosion inhibition.

In this study, N-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using pectin and organic amines as precursors. Their structures, corrosion inhibition performance, and mechanism for N80 steel in 1 M H2SO4 were systematically evaluated. The key findings are as follows:
(1)
UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy confirmed the photoluminescence properties of the carbon dots, and XPS analysis revealed that nitrogen atoms in the N-CDs exist as pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. The total nitrogen content and pyrrolic N fraction increased with the amine content in the nitrogen source.
(2)
Tafel and EIS analyses revealed that the synthesized N-CDs are mixed-type inhibitors with a predominant anodic inhibition effect. Corrosion inhibition efficiency depended strongly on the nitrogen source and N-CD concentration. At 200 mg/L, N-CDs (PHA-CD, PCA-CD, and PDA-CD) synthesized using monoamine, diamine, and triamine as nitrogen sources achieved maximum inhibition efficiencies of 75.3 %, 90.1 %, and 95.4 %, respectively. Consistent results were obtained from weight loss tests and surface analyses. This trend indicates that increasing the amine content in the nitrogen source enhances the total nitrogen content and pyrrolic N fraction in the N-CDs, thereby improving their corrosion inhibition performance.
(3)
The calculated K ads and −ΔG 0 ads values indicated that the adsorption behavior of N-CDs on carbon steel adheres to the Langmuir adsorption model, characterized by mixed chemisorption. This primarily involves the formation of coordination complexes between pyrrolic N and Fe, increasing the inhibitor’s surface coverage on carbon steel, thereby enhancing corrosion inhibition.
